# Release notes

Each release ships with a hand-written notes file at
`.github/release-notes/X.Y.Z.md`, where `X.Y.Z` matches the git tag
exactly. The notes file becomes the body of the GitHub release page
that `build-release.yml` creates when the tag is pushed.

## Why hand-written

Auto-generated notes (`gh release create --generate-notes`) emit a
flat list of every commit/PR. For a release that bundles dozens of
commits, that's noise rather than a release story. A short curated
file beats a long autogenerated wall every time. We are not (yet)
adopting a CHANGELOG.md or news-fragment tooling — each release is
its own file in this directory.

## How the workflow uses it

`.github/workflows/build-release.yml` checks the notes file for the
tag *before* running `uv build`. The check fails the workflow when
the file is:

- missing,
- empty (zero-byte), or
- still contains the template sentinel `TEMPLATE: replace this file`.

A clean check passes the file to `gh release create --notes-file`,
which uses its contents verbatim as the release body.

## Procedure for a new release

1. Bump `version` in `pyproject.toml` (e.g. `0.3.0` → `0.3.1`).

1. `uv lock` to pin the new version.

1. Copy the template: `cp .github/release-notes/_template.md .github/release-notes/0.3.1.md`.

1. Edit the new file. Replace the template sentinel and every
    `<placeholder>`. Keep sections relevant to the release; remove
    sections that don't apply.

1. Commit `pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock`, and the notes file in one
    commit (`chore: bump version to 0.3.1`).

1. Push to `main`.

1. Tag at the bump commit and push the tag:

    ```bash
    git tag -m 'v0.3.1' 0.3.1
    git push --tags
    ```

1. Watch the Actions tab. The workflow builds the wheel and creates
    the release page; the artifact filename is
    `tkc_lvlab-X.Y.Z-py3-none-any.whl`.

## Recovery: tag pushed without notes

If the workflow fails because the notes file is missing/empty/still
the template, the tag needs to point at a commit that does include
finished notes. The tag is moved, not deleted-and-forgotten:

```bash
# Delete the local tag and the remote tag.
git tag -d 0.3.1
git push origin :refs/tags/0.3.1

# Write/finish the notes file, commit it on main, push main.
$EDITOR .github/release-notes/0.3.1.md
git add .github/release-notes/0.3.1.md
git commit -m "docs: add 0.3.1 release notes"
git push

# Re-tag at the new HEAD (or at the specific commit that has the notes).
git tag -m 'v0.3.1' 0.3.1
git push --tags
```

The workflow re-runs on the new tag push and (if the notes file is
ready) creates the release.

## Tag is moved, not deleted-and-shipped

The workflow's `actions/checkout@v6` step uses
`ref: ${{ github.ref_name }}` — it checks out the **tag's commit**,
not `main`. If you push a tag that points at a commit *before* the
notes file was committed, the notes file won't be in the checked-out
tree even if it exists on `main`. That's by design: the release is
reproducible from the tagged commit alone.

## File conventions

- One file per release, named `X.Y.Z.md` matching the tag.
- Markdown. No frontmatter. Tables and code blocks render correctly
    on the GitHub release page.
- Headings start at `##` (the release page already has the version
    as `<h1>`).
- Keep the file short — bullet points over prose, deep links over
    paragraphs, exact commands over "see the docs."

## Old releases

Releases that pre-date this convention (anything before `0.3.0`)
do not have notes files in this directory. Their release pages
were created via `--generate-notes` and remain as-is on GitHub.
